You've got a clear idea of what would motivate you to take that first short walk. How about if we write down your plan and put it in a place where you'll see it often, like on your fridge or phone lock screen, to remind you of your commitment to trying something new?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.

I can see you've already made some progress by reducing your bidi smoking to 2 or 3 a day. What do you think triggered the increase to 25 bidis a day, and was there anything different about your life or habits back then that might have contributed to your higher smoking rate?

I think I smoked more back then because I was working in the government office, a lot of stress, and long hours. It was easier to have a bidi in my mouth while working. Now I've retired, things are a bit more relaxed, I'm not under the same pressure.

I think it's interesting how your bidis smoking seems to have been closely tied to the stress and pressure of your work. As you've retired and things have become more relaxed, you've been able to cut back on your smoking, which is really encouraging. What if we were to identify some of those stress-reducing activities you used to do at work and try to incorporate them into your daily routine now, to see if that could help you stay motivated to quit bidis?

That's a good idea, but I don't think it will be the same this time. I've tried relaxing and all that, it doesn't seem to make a difference. The last bidi I want to quit is the one I smoke as soon as I wake up. That's the hardest one to give up.

Now that we've discussed your struggles with morning cravings, I want to try something a bit different. Have you ever found yourself saying "yes" to a bidi just because someone offered it to you, or because it was there for you to grab, rather than genuinely wanting it?

Actually, yes. Sometimes when I'm visiting my older brother, he will offer me a bidi and I'll take it because it's just easier to say yes. He smokes a lot and it's just a habit we have. But, to be honest, it's not really about the bidi at that point, it's just a habit we share.

Now that you've acknowledged that saying "yes" to a bidi can be a habit, I'd like to practice a simple phrase with you to help you decline when your brother offers you a bidi - what do you think you might say to him instead?
I can say "no, thank you" or "I'm trying to quit". That's easy. The harder part is when I wake up in the morning, feeling like I need a bidi.

Now that you've identified that saying "yes" to a bidi is a habit you'd like to break, and you've come up with a simple phrase to decline when your brother offers you a bidi, what do you think might trigger your morning craving for a bidi, and is there anything we can do differently that morning to make it easier for you to resist the urge?

My morning craving is because it's just something I've been doing for so long. It's like a part of my routine. I think if I could do something else in the morning that gives me a similar feeling, that might help. Do you think trying some NRT, like those nicotine gum things, would help with that?

I understand that establishing a new morning routine might help you replace the craving for a bidi. Can you imagine doing something, like going for a short walk, as soon as you wake up to break the association of bidis with your morning routine?
Going for a short walk might be okay, I guess. But it's not like I've ever really enjoyed walking in the morning. And what if it's cold or rainy? Then what?

That's a valid concern about the weather. Let's think about how you could make going for a short walk more enjoyable for you, even when it's not ideal outside - would you be willing to try putting on a pair of warm socks or wearing a waterproof jacket to help make the experience more comfortable?

I suppose that could make a difference. I have a waterproof jacket that I used to use when I'd go for walks during the monsoon season, so it's just hanging around anyway. I haven't thought about using it again.
Now that you have a waterproof jacket that might make going for a walk more enjoyable, are you willing to try incorporating a short walk into your morning routine tomorrow and see how it goes?
I can try it. But what if I still want a bidi after the walk? That's not the same as quitting, is it?
That's a valid concern about still wanting a bidi after the walk, but let's break it down - if you can make it through the walk without smoking, that's a win, right?

Yeah, that is a win. I've been making it through most days without smoking, it's just that one bidi that's been sticking with me. If I can get through the morning walk without it, that's a good start.

You've been making some great progress, and I'm glad you're feeling positive about the morning walk idea. Can you think of a small reward you'd like to give yourself if you manage to get through the morning walk without smoking tomorrow?
That's not a bad idea. I could make myself a strong cup of tea, or some coffee, with some sugar like I used to have when I was working. That would be nice.
Now that you've thought of a reward for yourself, what do you think is the one thing that's still holding you back from quitting bidis for good?

I think it's just the habit now. It's not like I'm smoking because I need the nicotine, it's just that I've been doing it for so long that it feels like a part of me. But I've been doing better without the 20-odd bidis a day, so I'm hopeful that I can get through the last one or two.
You've been doing better without the 20-odd bidis a day, and that's a huge accomplishment. Would you be open to using a technique called "urge surfing" to help manage the cravings you still feel?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.

I can sense the weight of guilt and financial concern you're carrying about your smoking habit. Can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you're out with your family, especially when you see them looking at you or commenting about your smoking?
No, I don't usually think about that when I'm with my family. I focus on them, not my smoking. What I think about is the money I'm wasting, 4000 rupees a month, that's what I think about.

It sounds like the financial concern is a strong motivator for you. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you're feeling stressed, like when the money is low and you're worried about your family's needs?

I just get anxious, I suppose. Stress is stress, it feels the same, whether it's about money or traffic or whatever. The stress is there, and I need something to calm me down. Cigarettes do that for me, but I know they're not worth it.

You've mentioned that cigarettes calm you down when you're feeling anxious. Have you ever tried doing something else to calm yourself down, like taking a few deep breaths or going for a short walk, when you felt that anxious feeling rising up?

No, I don't really do that. I just... smoke. It's what I've always done. I mean, going for a walk sounds okay, I guess, but what's the point if I'm just going to get stuck in traffic or stressed about something else?

I understand you're skeptical about going for a walk if you'll just get stressed about something else. But what if you took a short walk just to get out of the situation that's causing you stress, rather than letting the stress dictate where you go?
I don't know... that sounds like a lot of work. To just walk somewhere, just to walk, not even thinking about what's stressing me out. That's hard to do. But, I suppose it's cheaper than smoking.

Let's see if we can break it down together. You feel that taking a short walk might be a bit of a challenge, especially if you're not used to it. 

Would you be willing to try taking a short walk with me as a thought experiment, just to imagine what it would be like, without actually going anywhere?
I'm not sure. I don't see what it would achieve.

You're worried that taking a short walk won't address the underlying stress that's making you want to smoke. Do you think if you had a specific goal or intention in mind for that walk, like taking a few deep breaths or focusing on your surroundings, it might make it more worthwhile for you?
I suppose it's worth trying. But what's with all these deep breaths and stuff, can't I just focus on... I don't know, getting to the next delivery quicker, something practical like that?

You're not sure about the deep breathing part, but you're open to trying a short walk with a specific goal in mind, like getting to the next delivery quicker. Can you imagine how it might feel to take that short walk, focusing on your surroundings and getting to your destination, rather than letting stress dictate your actions?
Yeah, okay, I can imagine it. It sounds... easier, I guess. I mean, I'm already walking to get to the next delivery, so why not just try to enjoy the walk more, you know?

You're starting to see the potential in taking a short walk to manage your stress. Now, what do you think would happen if you incorporated this walk into your routine immediately after meals, like right after you finish eating?

That's a good idea, because after meals is when I usually crave a cigarette. If I just took a short walk instead, it might help me break the habit. Plus, it's a pretty short walk, just from my delivery van to the restaurant, back. It's not like I'm exerting myself or anything.

You're thinking a short walk could be a good replacement for smoking after meals. Can you imagine putting a small reminder on your phone or leaving a note on your dashboard to take that walk after every meal, so it becomes a consistent habit?

Yeah, that sounds like a good idea. A note on my dashboard or a reminder on my phone would be easy to do. And if I can make it a habit, maybe I can cut down on cigarettes, or stop altogether. The money I save would be nice too.

You're feeling hopeful about breaking the cigarette habit with a short walk after meals. How about taking it a step further and planning to reward yourself with something you enjoy, like a favorite snack or activity, after a few days of successfully walking instead of smoking?

A reward sounds good. Something like a nice cup of chai or a movie with my family would be nice. If I can stick to it for a few days, the reward would give me something to look forward to. Yeah, that might help.

You're thinking that a reward like a nice cup of chai or a movie with your family would motivate you to stick to the new habit. Can you imagine how you'll feel when you achieve that milestone, and how you'll celebrate it?

I can imagine it. My family will be happy, and we'll have a nice time together. And I'll feel good about myself, you know? Like I've actually done something for my family, besides just throwing money at our problems.

You're feeling good about the idea of a reward and how it will motivate you. What do you think would be the best way to track your progress and stay on track with your new habit of walking after meals?
I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.
You say you're not a "real" smoker, but those instances still trigger you to smoke. How do you think we could make those social situations a little less tempting for you when you're trying to quit?

I guess I just feel like I need a cigarette to fit in, you know? My friends don't really drink without smoking, and I don't want to be left out. But I'm worried about my asthma, it's getting really bad. Do you think there's a way to deal with those situations without smoking?

It sounds like feeling left out is a big concern for you in social situations. Would it be helpful to practice politely saying no to a cigarette when your friends offer one, so you can remind yourself why you quit in the first place?

Yeah, maybe just saying no would help. But it's hard, because my friends are all smokers too, and it feels like they'll think I'm annoying or judging them if I don't join in. I don't want to be that person.

You're worried about your friends judging you if you say no to a cigarette. Do you think having a friend who already knows you're trying to quit and can support you might make a big difference in those situations?

That's actually a good idea. One of my friends, Emily, has been trying to quit for ages, and I've been sort of supportive of her, I guess. Maybe I could talk to her about it and get her opinion on quitting. That would make it feel less weird, like I'm not just quitting by myself.

It sounds like talking to Emily about quitting might give you the support and validation you need in social situations. Would it be helpful if we role-play a conversation with Emily, so you can practice saying no to a cigarette and feeling more confident in your decision to quit?

I don't know...it feels a little silly, practicing like that. But I guess it couldn't hurt. Just to make sure I can sound convincing when I tell her I'm quitting, in case she tries to convince me to join her for a cigarette later.
Let's practice that conversation with Emily in a low-stakes way, like a mock phone call, to help you feel more comfortable and confident. Would you be okay with that?
Yeah, okay. That sounds kind of helpful, actually. What's the scenario supposed to be?

Let's make the scenario a casual get-together with friends at a party, where you're offered a cigarette and you need to say no. Would you be able to think of a simple phrase to decline the offer that feels authentic to you?
Like, "I'm trying to cut back on smoking, but thanks for offering." Yeah, that feels okay, I think. But what if they pressure me?

You're thinking of a simple phrase to decline the offer, which is great. What do you think would happen if you were to say that phrase, "I'm trying to cut back on smoking, but thanks for offering," to someone who's really pushing you to smoke?
They might just laugh it off and say something like, "Oh, come on, it's just one cigarette!" But I don't think it's just about that one cigarette...it's about not smoking at all.

That's a great insight into how you feel about it not being just about one cigarette. Now, if someone did laugh it off and try to pressure you further, would you be willing to say something like, "I appreciate your understanding, but I really need to stick to my goal of quitting smoking"?

Yeah, that sounds okay, I think. Like, a little firmer, but still polite. I wouldn't want to come across as being a total buzzkill or anything. But at the same time, I need to be firm with myself about quitting.

You're starting to feel more confident in saying no to cigarettes and being firm with yourself about quitting. Can you imagine using that phrase, "I appreciate your understanding, but I really need to stick to my goal of quitting smoking," in a situation where you're feeling really tempted to smoke, like when someone offers you a cigarette at a party?

I think so...yeah, I could see myself saying that. It feels like a real "me" thing to say. I still don't feel totally addicted or anything, but I guess I'm starting to realize that it's not just about the smoking, it's about being able to take care of myself and my asthma.

You've been considering that phrase and feeling more confident about using it in tempting situations. What do you think would help you remember to use that phrase when you're in the midst of a stressful or tempting situation, like a party with friends?

I think if I remind myself why I'm quitting - my asthma, feeling healthier - it might help me stay focused on that goal and use the phrase without thinking twice. Or maybe I could write it down or put a note somewhere as a reminder, like on my fridge or something.

I love that you're thinking of ways to remind yourself of your reasons for quitting. Before we think about reminders, can you tell me more about what it's like for you to write down your reasons and what you hope will happen when you look at them?

When I think about writing down my reasons for quitting, I imagine it'll be a way to get everything clear in my head. Like, putting it on paper, so to speak. And when I look at it, I hope it'll be like a little kick in the pants, reminding me of why I'm doing this. I want to be able to see that I'm not just quitting for myself, but for my health and for my future.

You've been imagining that writing down your reasons will help you get clear and motivated. Would you be open to starting a journal or notebook to write down your reasons for quitting, and see how it makes you feel?
